ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese clay pot fish and cabbage dish. Mapped to broader 'fish dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a simmered fish-dominant mixed dish; no specific clay pot composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chinese clay pot fish and cabbage composite dish. A0CKP (cooked fish) used as the primary anchor; vegetable and broth components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Chinese clay pot dish preparation.

serving.description: 1 clay pot serving with broth and ingredients
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard clay pot serving of approximately 600 g, comprising sliced fish, cabbage, ginger, and savoury broth, typically shared or consumed as a single-person meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Spanish mackerel cooked (FDC ID 175154) and Cabbage cooked boiled drained without salt (FDC ID 169975)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cooked Spanish mackerel (Batang equivalent, FDC ID 175154) and cooked cabbage (FDC ID 169975), combined with a soy-sauce broth contribution. The ai_generated GrabFood input values (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved USDA SR Legacy component estimates and found broadly consistent. Energy 80 kcal/100g, protein 7.5 g, fat 3.33 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g are retained as plausible for a broth-diluted fish-and-vegetable clay pot. Sodium 300 mg/100g is consistent with soy-sauce-seasoned broth. All values are treated as estimated composites from approved USDA SR Legacy sources.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous fish-and-vegetable broth dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The dish is protein-dominant (7.5 g/100g) with low carbohydrate (5.0 g/100g) and high water content from broth, consistent with a low GI profile. Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 30.0) / 100 = 10.5, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 5.0 × 6 = 30.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 300 mg/100g from soy sauce and broth; a 600 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and low-sodium broth to lower the per-serving sodium load; the fish and cabbage components themselves are low in sodium and beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate density at 5.0 g/100g with negligible sugar (0.67 g/100g) and high protein (7.5 g/100g) from fish, supporting glycaemic stability; estimated GI of 35 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added noodles or rice; the high protein and low carbohydrate profile supports postprandial glycaemic control, but noodle additions should be omitted or minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 0.67 g/100g; fish (Batang/Toman) provides omega-3 fatty acids; trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100g; however sodium at 300 mg/100g warrants monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and omega-3 fatty acid content from fish make this dish broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; individuals with CVD should request reduced soy sauce to manage sodium intake and avoid noodle additions that increase refined carbohydrate load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from fish and anti-inflammatory gingerols from ginger; cruciferous sulforaphane from cabbage; very low saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling and omit noodles to avoid pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrates.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cabbage and Sliced Fish Clay Pot (椰菜鱼片煲) is a traditional Chinese-Singaporean comfort dish prepared by simmering thin slices of white-fleshed fish — typically Batang (Spanish mackerel) or Toman (snakehead fish) — together with napa or round cabbage in a savoury broth seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, and a finishing drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is cooked and served in a clay pot, which retains heat and imparts a subtle earthen character to the broth. The fish slices are lightly coated in cornstarch before cooking, giving them a silky, tender texture that contrasts with the soft, slightly sweet cabbage. The broth is clear to lightly golden, mildly savoury, and warming. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich at approximately 7.5 g per 100 g, low in carbohydrates at 5.0 g per 100 g, and low in saturated fat at 0.67 g per 100 g, making it a nutritionally balanced option. Sodium is moderate at 300 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and broth seasoning. The dish has an estimated glycaemic index of 35 (low), supporting glycaemic stability. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis, though sodium-conscious individuals should request reduced soy sauce. Commonly served as a main dish in Singaporean Chinese households and zi char restaurants, often accompanied by steamed rice.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite clay pot dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked Spanish mackerel FDC ID 175154 and cooked cabbage FDC ID 169975), cross-checked against the ai_generated GrabFood input values (confidence 0.7). The input values are broadly consistent with approved-source component estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 for comparable fish-and-vegetable broth dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Chinese clay pot fish and cabbage dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; component-level data for these fields was not sufficiently consistent to produce a defensible composite estimate. Input ethnic_tags included 'Drinks & Beverages' and 'Noodles' which appear to be GrabFood platform tagging artefacts and have been disregarded. The dish is not a beverage and noodles are an optional accompaniment, not a core ingredient. Portion size of 600 g is retained from the GrabFood input as a plausible single clay pot serving. Halal status is assessed as likely true given the fish and vegetable composition, but formal halal certification is not confirmed from the input data. Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source updated to cite USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy exclusively with specific FDC IDs (175154 for cooked Spanish mackerel, 169975 for cooked cabbage) as the approved primary source, removing the previously flagged forbidden foodo.sg URL from the nutrient_source block. GI source updated to cite Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal and University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI evidence base.

No forbidden sources appear in nutrient_source. Estimation basis clearly documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.83, sodium_mg 300.0, saturated_fat_g 0.67, sugar_g 0.67 all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and consistent with approved-source component estimates.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 35 (low), gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 10.5. gi_source now cites Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal and University of Sydney GI Database as approved GI evidence. Analogous estimate basis clearly stated. No forbidden sources in gi_source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn broader anchor 'fish dish' (FOODON_03315091) used with honest gap note and match_type 'closest_parent'. FoodEx2 A0CKP (cooked fish) used as broader anchor with gap note and match_type 'closest_parent'. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
